MEETING DATE:
December 06, 2021 
SUBJECT:
National Opioid Litigation and Settlement Agreements – Participation Authorization
RECOMMENDATION:
Authorize the City Attorney to take all necessary steps and execute appropriate documents to participate in Opioid 
Settlement Agreements with Distributors and Janssen and agree to implementing State Subdivision Agreements. 
BACKGROUND:
On July 21, 2021, two proposed nationwide settlement agreements were announced by several State Attorneys General, 
four major defendants and others totaling $26 billion to resolve opioid lawsuits filed around the county. The City has until 
January 2, 2022, to decide whether to participate in these settlement agreements, the payouts for which will occur over a 
multi-year period. 
Signing participation agreements will help direct settlement funds towards opioid mitigation efforts locally. If the City opts 
into the settlements, it must release its claims against the opioid distributors and manufacturers that are participating in 
the settlements. The State of California and San Mateo County have both joined the settlements. 
If the City does not sign the participation agreements, then the funds will go to the State, but the total amount the State 
receives may be reduced since it is based on participating cities and counties. By not opting in, the City preserves the 
ability to litigate against the opioid distributors and manufacturers. 
One settlement agreement is for $21 billion and is with three opioid distributors – Cardinal Health, McKesson, and 
AmerisourceBergen (hereinafter, the “Distributors”). These funds would be distributed to states and local governments 
over an 18-year period. The second settlement agreement is for $5 billion and is with the opioid manufacturer Janssen 
Pharmaceuticals, Inc., which is owned by Johnson and Johnson (collectively, “J&J”). These funds would be distributed over 
no more than nine years. 
California will receive about 9.9% of the total $26 billion under both settlement agreements, which amounts to roughly 
$2.26 billion. This amount accounts for population and the severity of harm caused by the opioid epidemic. Of the funds 
going to subdivisions of the State of California, the overwhelming bulk (at least 85%) is restricted to funding future 
abatement efforts, and other uses are disfavored. Approximately 70% of these funds will go directly to cities and counties 
as abatement funds. Then, 15% will go to the State, and the remaining 15% to litigating entities. Under Appendix 1 to the 
Proposed California State – Subdivision Agreement, the City’s share of the 70% abatement fund is approximately 0.052%. 
We understand that the City could receive approximately $820,000 in total, to be paid out over 18 years for the 
Distributors settlement and over 9 years for the J&J settlement. The exact amount will depend on how many jurisdictions 
participate. Under an incentive-based formula, the more jurisdictions that participate, the more in abatement funds that 
1299 of 1300

--- page 1300 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
California and participating subdivisions will receive. 
These funds will go to the City if it joins the settlements and opts for direct payment. If the City joins the settlements but 
does not opt for direct payment, those funds will go to San Mateo County. The City can make an election any time prior to 
a settlement fund payment date to receive funds directly. But this election will occur only after it has joined the 
settlements. Funds could begin to flow to states and local governments as early as April 2022. Staff will return to the 
Council for a decision as to whether direct payment to the City or distribution of funds to the County is appropriate. 
Funds going directly to participating states and subdivisions must be used for pre-approved purposes designed to help 
abate the opioid epidemic. These include a wide range of intervention, treatment, education, and recovery services. If the 
City receives direct payment it is obligated to report on the use of the funds, which must be used primarily for opioid 
abatement.
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budget impact for taking this action as it involves a decision whether to participate in settlement agreements. 
Staff will return to the Council later for direction as to whether the City should receive direct payments or authorize 
payment of the settlement funds to San Mateo County.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This proposed action is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not 
result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
